section.title: God the Righteous Judge
section.sectionRationale: A psalm of Asaph cast as a covenant lawsuit: God the Judge summons heaven and earth as witnesses, then addresses first His covenant people about the true nature of worship, and then the wicked about their empty profession. It teaches that God needs nothing from His people's sacrifices; He desires a thankful, obedient heart.
section.thesis: God, the divine Judge, does not need Israel's sacrifices for His own sustenance; He desires a thankful and obedient heart, and He will one day judge both covenant people and the wicked according to truth.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the theophany where God summons all the earth to judgment; His word to the covenant people about true worship; His rebuke of the wicked's hypocrisy; and a closing call to consider this, or be torn apart, paired with the promise of salvation to the one who walks rightly.

units.label: The Mighty One, God the LORD, speaks
units.note: the theophany that summons the earth

verses.text:
The Mighty One, {{elohim|God}} the LORD, speaks and summons the earth 
from where the sun rises to where it sets.
From Zion, perfect in beauty, God shines forth.

Our God approaches and will not be silent! Consuming fire precedes Him, 
and a tempest rages around Him.
He summons the heavens above, and the earth, that He may judge His people:
“Gather to Me My saints, who made a {{berith|covenant}} with Me by sacrifice.”
And the heavens proclaim His righteousness, for God Himself is Judge. Selah

units.label: I do not rebuke you for your sacrifices
units.note: the word to the covenant people about true worship

verses.text:
“Hear, O My people, and I will speak, O Israel, and I will testify against you: 
I am God, your God.
I do not rebuke you for your sacrifices, and your burnt offerings are ever before Me.
I have no need for a bull from your stall or goats from your pens,
for every beast of the forest is Mine, the cattle on a thousand hills.
I know every bird in the mountains, and the creatures of the field are Mine.
If I were hungry, I would not tell you, for the world is Mine, and the fullness thereof.
Do I eat the flesh of bulls, or drink the blood of goats?
Sacrifice a {{todah|thank offering}} to God, and fulfill your vows to the Most High.
Call upon Me in the day of trouble; I will deliver you, and you will honor Me.”

units.label: What right have you to recite My statutes
units.note: the rebuke of the wicked's hypocrisy

verses.text:
To the wicked, however, God says, “What right have you to recite My statutes 
and to bear My covenant on your lips?
For you hate My instruction and cast My words behind you.
When you see a thief, you befriend him, and throw in your lot with adulterers.
You unleash your mouth for evil and unharness your tongue for deceit.
You sit and malign your brother; you slander your own mother’s son.
You have done these things, and I kept silent; you thought I was just like you. But now I rebuke you and accuse you to your face.

units.label: He who sacrifices a thank offering honors Me

verses.text:
Now consider this, you who forget God, lest I tear you to pieces, 
with no one to rescue you:

He who sacrifices a thank offering honors Me, and to him who rights his way, 
I will show the salvation of God.”

elohim.gloss: Verse 1 stacks three names for God together, the Mighty One, God, the LORD, an emphatic opening that leaves no doubt this is the sovereign covenant God Himself who comes to judge, not a lesser power.

berith.gloss: Covenant, a binding relationship sealed by sacrifice. God gathers His saints as those bound to Him by covenant, the same word used of His covenants with Abraham, Israel at Sinai, and finally the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood.

todah.gloss: Thank offering, an offering of gratitude rather than mere obligation. God repeats this word at the psalm's center and its close, verses 14 and 23, insisting that a thankful heart, not the sacrifice's material value, is what truly honors Him.

basic.context: Asaph pictures God arriving as Judge in a blazing theophany, summoning heaven and earth as witnesses against His own covenant people. God is not angry that Israel offers sacrifices; He is angry that they think He needs them, as though He were hungry and dependent on their rituals. What He wants is a thankful heart and a life that calls on Him honestly in trouble. Then He turns to the wicked, who recite His statutes with their lips while living in complicity with thieves and adulterers, and warns them plainly.
basic.hermeneutics: This is covenant lawsuit language, common in the prophets, where God brings a legal case against His own people for hollow religion. It should not be read as rejecting sacrifice itself, God ordained the sacrificial system, but as correcting the notion that ritual alone, without a grateful and obedient heart, can satisfy Him.
basic.originalLanguages: The opening verse stacks three names for God, El, Elohim, YHWH, an unmistakable declaration that the sovereign covenant Lord Himself is speaking. He calls His people to Him by berith, covenant, and twice insists that a todah, a thank offering from a grateful heart, is what truly honors Him, not the material sacrifice by itself.
basic.typology: God's insistence that He needs nothing from animal sacrifice, only a thankful and obedient heart, anticipates the once for all sacrifice of Christ, after which the only offering left for His people to bring is the sacrifice of praise and a life rightly lived.
basic.christ: Jesus is the true and final Judge this psalm anticipates, before whom all peoples will one day stand, John 5:22, Matthew 25:31-32. He also fulfills what this psalm demands beyond ritual: a life and death that is itself the perfect thank offering, so that His people now offer, through Him, a sacrifice of praise, the fruit of lips that confess His name, Hebrews 13:15.
basic.god: God is shown as utterly self-sufficient, needing nothing from His creatures, yet still desiring relationship with them, calling for gratitude and honesty in trouble rather than empty religious performance. He is also the impartial Judge who holds His own covenant people to a higher account than anyone else.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 15:22 says to obey is better than sacrifice. Hosea 6:6 declares God desires mercy, not sacrifice. Hebrews 13:15 calls believers to offer a continual sacrifice of praise through Jesus. John 5:22 shows the Father has given all judgment to the Son.
basic.oneStory: God, the righteous Judge, needs nothing His people can offer Him; He desires a thankful, honest heart. That desire finds its answer once for all in Christ, the perfect thank offering, through whom His people now offer the sacrifice of praise while they wait for Him to come again as Judge of all the earth.

context.literary: A covenant lawsuit psalm in three parts: a theophany summoning heaven and earth as witnesses, verses 1-6; an address to the covenant people correcting their view of sacrifice, verses 7-15; and an address to the wicked exposing their hypocrisy, verses 16-21, closing with a warning and a promise, verses 22-23.
context.historical: Attributed to Asaph, a chief musician under David, ministering in a period when the sacrificial system was central to Israel's worship. The psalm confronts a real danger, that formal, correct ritual can mask a heart estranged from God and complicit with evildoers.
context.canonical: Stands with the prophets, 1 Samuel 15:22, Isaiah 1:11-17, Hosea 6:6, Micah 6:6-8, in insisting that God desires obedience and mercy over mere ritual, a theme Jesus takes up directly in His own teaching on true worship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct a wrong view of sacrifice among God's covenant people, that ritual alone satisfies God, and to warn the hypocritical wicked that reciting God's statutes without living by them will not escape judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific covenant lawsuit scene; it prescribes an abiding truth for every generation, that God desires a thankful, honest heart and a life that matches its profession, not empty religious performance.

debates.question: Does this psalm reject the sacrificial system itself, or only its abuse?

views.label: It corrects the abuse, not the institution
views.case: God Himself ordained the sacrificial system; the psalm's complaint is that Israel treated sacrifice as feeding or obligating God, not that sacrifice itself was wrong or unnecessary.

views.label: It anticipates the obsolescence of the whole sacrificial pattern
views.case: The sheer force of God saying He has no need of bulls or goats, paired with prophetic texts making similar points, can be read as preparing the way for a sacrificial system that would itself be fulfilled and ended in something greater.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that true worship was always meant to flow from a thankful, obedient heart; the sacrificial system, whether corrected or eventually fulfilled, always pointed beyond itself to the true and greater sacrifice in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The opening verse piles up three divine names, El, Elohim, YHWH, an emphatic declaration that the sovereign covenant God Himself is speaking. He gathers His people by berith, covenant, and twice calls for a todah, a thank offering from a grateful heart, insisting this, not the material sacrifice, is what truly honors Him.

typology.type: God's declaration that He needs no sacrifice for sustenance, verses 9-13
typology.fulfillment: The once for all sacrifice of Christ, after which His people offer only the sacrifice of praise, the fruit of lips that confess His name, Hebrews 13:15.

typology.type: God as Judge summoning heaven and earth as witnesses, verses 1-6
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, to whom the Father has given all judgment, and before whom all peoples will one day stand, John 5:22, Matthew 25:31-32.

study.christ: Jesus is the true and final Judge this psalm anticipates, the one to whom the Father has given all judgment, John 5:22, before whom all nations will be gathered, Matthew 25:31-32. He also fulfills what this psalm ultimately demands beyond ritual: His own perfect life and sacrificial death mean His people now offer, through Him, a sacrifice of praise, the fruit of lips that confess His name, Hebrews 13:15, rather than trusting in ritual performance alone.
study.god: God is utterly self-sufficient, needing nothing from His creatures, yet He still desires relationship, calling for gratitude and honesty in trouble rather than hollow religious performance. He is also the impartial Judge who holds His own covenant people to as high, or higher, an account than anyone else.

crossReferences.note: To obey is better than sacrifice, the same priority this psalm insists on.
God desires mercy and the knowledge of God, not mere sacrifice.
Believers are called to offer a continual sacrifice of praise to God through Jesus.

study.oneStory: God, the righteous Judge, needs nothing His people can offer Him; He desires a thankful, honest heart. That desire finds its answer once for all in Christ, the perfect and final sacrifice, through whom His people now offer the sacrifice of praise while they wait for Him to come again as Judge of all the earth.

sermon.title: Call Upon Me in the Day of Trouble and I Will Deliver You
sermon.why: Piper works through Psalm 50's picture of God summoning heaven and earth as judge, and its call to true worship out of gratitude and honest dependence, showing the difference between empty ritual and a heart that truly calls on God in the day of trouble.

quiz.q: What kind of psalm is Psalm 50, in its overall shape?

options.text: A covenant lawsuit, with God as Judge summoning witnesses and addressing His people and the wicked in turn.
options.note: The theophany, summons of witnesses, and direct address to two groups match the classic covenant lawsuit pattern found also in the prophets.

options.text: A simple song of personal thanksgiving.
options.note: Thank offerings are discussed, but the psalm's overall shape is a legal summons and judgment scene, not a private thanksgiving song.

options.text: A love song for a royal wedding.
options.note: That describes Psalm 45, not Psalm 50.

options.text: A historical narrative retelling the exodus.
options.note: The exodus is not the subject here; the psalm is a courtroom-like scene set in the present, addressing worship and hypocrisy.

quiz.q: Why does God say He does not rebuke Israel for their sacrifices, yet still correct them (verses 8-13)?

options.text: Because they wrongly imagined God needed their offerings for sustenance, when the world and everything in it is already His.
options.note: Verses 9-13 make this explicit: God owns every animal and needs nothing from Israel's rituals.

options.text: Because their offerings were the wrong species of animal.
options.note: The text says nothing about incorrect species; the problem is the wrong view of why sacrifice is offered.

options.text: Because sacrifice itself had just been abolished.
options.note: The psalm does not abolish sacrifice; it corrects a wrong understanding of it.

options.text: Because they were not offering enough sacrifices.
options.note: Quantity is not the issue raised; the issue is the heart and understanding behind the sacrifice.

quiz.q: What does God specifically call for instead of, or alongside, ritual sacrifice (verses 14-15)?

options.text: A thank offering, fulfilled vows, and honestly calling upon God in the day of trouble.
options.note: Verses 14 and 15 state this directly as what truly honors God.

options.text: Total abandonment of the temple and priesthood.
options.note: The psalm does not call for this; it calls for a right heart within the existing covenant relationship.

options.text: Silence and the cessation of all prayer.
options.note: The opposite is true; God explicitly invites His people to call upon Him.

options.text: Larger and more costly sacrifices than before.
options.note: The emphasis is not on size or cost but on gratitude and honest dependence on God.

quiz.q: What specific hypocrisy does God rebuke in the wicked (verses 16-21)?

options.text: Reciting God's statutes and covenant with their lips while befriending thieves, adulterers, and slandering their own family.
options.note: Verses 16-20 list exactly this contradiction between profession and practice.

options.text: Refusing to attend temple worship at all.
options.note: The rebuke concerns those who do speak of God's covenant, but live in contradiction to it, not mere absence from worship.

options.text: Worshiping foreign gods openly.
options.note: The text does not describe idolatry here; it describes hypocritical profession alongside complicity with evil.

options.text: Giving too much money to the poor.
options.note: This is not mentioned; the rebuke concerns hypocrisy, not excessive generosity.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's demand for true worship find its fulfillment in Christ?

options.text: Christ offers the once for all sacrifice, after which His people offer God the sacrifice of praise, the fruit of lips that confess His name.
options.note: Hebrews 13:15 directly connects this pattern of praise-as-sacrifice to what Christ has accomplished.

options.text: Christ teaches that no sacrifice or offering of any kind is ever appropriate.
options.note: Scripture continues to call for offerings of praise and generosity even after Christ; the point is the heart behind them.

options.text: Christ replaces thankfulness with strict ritual obligation.
options.note: This reverses the psalm's and the New Testament's emphasis on grateful, willing worship over mere obligation.

options.text: Christ simply repeats the Old Testament sacrificial system without change.
options.note: Hebrews shows Christ's sacrifice as final and complete, not a mere repetition of the old pattern.

questions: God says He does not need Israel's sacrifices, because everything already belongs to Him. Where might you be treating your service to God as something He needs from you, rather than a grateful response to what He has already given?

God calls for a thank offering and honest calling upon Him in the day of trouble, rather than empty ritual. What would it look like this week to bring your real troubles honestly to God rather than performing religious routine?
The wicked in this psalm recite God's covenant with their lips while living in contradiction to it. Where is there a gap between what you profess about God and how you actually treat others?

God says He kept silent while the wicked sinned, and they mistakenly thought He was just like them. How does remembering that God's patience is not indifference change how you think about unaddressed sin in your own life?

The psalm closes with a promise, salvation shown to the one who rights his way. How does knowing Christ has already offered the perfect sacrifice change your motivation to live rightly, from fear to gratitude?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew stacks three names for God together, El, Elohim, YHWH, an emphatic construction some versions render as 'The Mighty One, God, the LORD' to preserve the force of all three names appearing in sequence.
Made a covenant with Me by sacrifice recalls the sealing of the Sinai covenant in Exodus 24, where sacrifice ratified the relationship between God and His people.

Thank offering renders todah, a specific category of peace offering, Leviticus 7:12-15, offered in gratitude rather than for atonement, fitting the psalm's emphasis on a grateful rather than merely obligatory sacrifice.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 50:1-23. One em dash present in the raw BSB text at verse 10 was converted to a comma. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of desiringgod.org (series page and confirmed scripture tag Psalm 50).
